coredata.dc:title: Hyaluronic acid and carbon dots-gated hollow mesoporous silica for redox and enzyme-triggered targeted drug delivery and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Qinfu
Wang, Shengyu
Yang, Yang
Li, Xian
Di, Donghua
Zhang, Chungang
Jiang, Tongying
Wang, Siling
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a redox and enzyme dual-stimuli responsive drug delivery system (DDS) with tracking function (HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA) based on carbon dots capped hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (HMSN) has been developed for targeted drug delivery. The positively charged CDPEI nanoparticles prepared by polyethylenimine (PEI) were grafted on the pore openings of HMSN through disulfide bonds and were used as “gatekeepers” to trap the drugs within the hollow cavity. The hyaluronic acid (HA), a natural polysaccharide, was further grafted on the surface of HMSN to realize targeted drug delivery, controlled drug release and improved the stability. Doxorubicin (DOX) was chosen as a model drug due to its wide clinical application. In vitro drug release profiles demonstrated that DOX-loaded HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA exhibited redox and enzyme dual-responsive drug release property. In addition, the prepared HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA exhibited excellent fluorescent properties and biocompatibility. Confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) and flow cytometry (FCM) illustrated that HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA exhibited a higher cellular uptake via the CD44 receptor-mediated endocytosis by CD44-receptor over-expressed A549 cells than NIH-3T3 (receptor-negative) cells, leading to higher cytotoxicity against A549 cells than NIH 3T3 cells. This work suggested an exploration of dual-stimuli responsive as well as real-time imaging targeted drug delivery system based on HMSN and the prepared HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA could be a promising platform for cancer therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hollow mesoporous silica
Dual-stimuli responsive
Carbon dots
Hyaluronic acid
Doxorubicin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Drug loading process and the dual-stimuli responsive drug release
The loading efficiency and redox and enzyme dual-triggered drug release
In vitro targeting analysis of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (A) and (B), XPS spectrum (C), and XRD pattern (D) of CDPEI.

(A) UV/Vis absorption, PL excitation and emission spectra of CDPEI in aqueous solutions. The insets are photos of CDPEI in bright field (left) and 365nm UV light (right); and (B) PL emission spectra of CDPEI at different excitation wavelengths in water.
TEM of (A) HMSN and (B) HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA nanoparticles.
The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distributions of HMSN and HMSN-SH.

The corresponding zeta potentials (A) and the FT-IR spectra (B) of HMSN-SH, HMSN-SS-COOH, HMSN-SS-CDPEI and HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA and the TGA curves (C) of HMSN-SH, HMSN-SS-COOH, HMSN-SS-CDPEI and HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA and images (D) of HMSN and HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA dispersed in distilled water and pH7.4 PBS with the concentration of 3mg/mL.
Cumulative release profiles of DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA in pH5.0 PBS (A) and pH7.4 PBS (B) under different conditions.

(A) The MFI of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA and HMSN-SS-CDPEI was measured by FCM in A549 and NIH-3T3 cells; and (B) intracellular MFI of blank cells (Blank), cells with HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA, cell pretreated with HA before the addition of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA and cells with HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA kept at 4°C in A549 cells were analyzed by FCM.
CLSM images of CD44 receptor-mediated endocytosis of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA by A549 and NIH-3T3 cells.
The cell viability of DOX-loaded HMSN nanoparticles at different concentrations of DOX in (A) A549 cells and (B) NIH-3T3 cells for 48h.
(A) Drug loading process of DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA and (B) CD44 receptor-mediated cellular uptake and dual-stimuli responsive drug release in response to GSH and HAase.
The drug release mechanism of DOX from DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA nanoparticles.
The N2 adsorption–desorption parameters of different functionalized HMSN nanoparticles.
IC50 value for free DOX, DOX/HMSN-SH and DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA samples for inhibiting growth of A549 cells and NIH-3T3 cells after 48h incubation.

head.ce:title: Hyaluronic acid and carbon dots-gated hollow mesoporous silica for redox and enzyme-triggered targeted drug delivery and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenyang, Liaoning Province 110016, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Liaoning University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, No. 77, Life One Road, DD port, Dalian 116600, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning University of Traditional Chinese Medicine
sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: No. 77, Life One Road, DD port

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a redox and enzyme dual-stimuli responsive drug delivery system (DDS) with tracking function (HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA) based on carbon dots capped hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (HMSN) has been developed for targeted drug delivery. The positively charged CDPEI nanoparticles prepared by polyethylenimine (PEI) were grafted on the pore openings of HMSN through disulfide bonds and were used as “gatekeepers” to trap the drugs within the hollow cavity. The hyaluronic acid (HA), a natural polysaccharide, was further grafted on the surface of HMSN to realize targeted drug delivery, controlled drug release and improved the stability. Doxorubicin (DOX) was chosen as a model drug due to its wide clinical application. In vitro drug release profiles demonstrated that DOX-loaded HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA exhibited redox and enzyme dual-responsive drug release property. In addition, the prepared HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA exhibited excellent fluorescent properties and biocompatibility. Confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) and flow cytometry (FCM) illustrated that HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA exhibited a higher cellular uptake via the CD44 receptor-mediated endocytosis by CD44-receptor over-expressed A549 cells than NIH-3T3 (receptor-negative) cells, leading to higher cytotoxicity against A549 cells than NIH 3T3 cells. This work suggested an exploration of dual-stimuli responsive as well as real-time imaging targeted drug delivery system based on HMSN and the prepared HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA could be a promising platform for cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decades, considerable efforts have been made to improve the therapeutic effect and reduce the side effect of the anticancer drug by targeted drug delivery. Although great achievements have been made, there are still a few challenges for the targeted drug delivery system (DDS). One of them is the premature drug leakage during transporting process. Recently, the stimuli-responsive DDS has aroused great interest as a promising solution for the above problem. Stimuli including pH [1], redox potential [2], temperature, light [3] and enzymes [4] could trigger the drug release at the specific target tissues.

As is known to us, intracellular fluids contain higher concentration of GSH (1–10mM) than extracellular fluids (2–10μM) does. And the levels of cytosolic GSH in tumor cells are at least 4 times higher than that in normal cells which could be used an appealing intracellular stimulus. Therefore, disulfide bonds are frequently used in the design of redox-responsive DDS since it is steady in extracellular fluids but easily broken in intracellular fluid containing high concentration of GSH. According to the physiological characteristics of tumor microenvironment, both redox potential and enzymes have received tremendous attention for designing dual-stimuli responsive DDS [5,6]. Hyaluronic acid (HA), a natural anionic polysaccharide with good biocompatibility is also known as a major extracellular matrix of connective tissues [7,8], and could be served as an excellent capping agent for reducing the premature drug leakage due to its large molecular size. Besides, HA could be easily degraded into low molecular weight fragments in some tumor tissues abundant in hyaluronidase (HAase). Moreover, HA receptor was overexpressed in many types of tumor and could act as a targeting ligand for CD44 receptors. Taking all the advantages of HA into account, it was exploited in this DDS to enhance the intracellular uptake and improve the therapeutic efficacy [9].

In recent years, the stimuli-responsive targeted DDS based on mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSN) have been explored extensively due to its stable mesoporous structure, tunable pore volume and size, high surface area and easily functionalized surface. Compared with the conventional MSN, hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (HMSN) had a hollow cavity, which endow them with a higher drug loading efficiency. Therefore, the HMSN had a promising application in drug delivery. Various gatekeepers based on HMSN, such as polymers [10], biomacromolecules [11], macrocyclic compounds [12] and inorganic nanoparticles [13] have been extensively studied to control the drug release from the nanocarriers. Among the various gatekeepers, carbon dots (CDs), a novel fluorescent nanomaterial, have been widely applied as a gatekeeper owing to their appropriate diameter and excellent fluorescence characteristics. In addition, these functional CDs are superior to traditional heavy-metal based quantum dots for their good water solubility, excellent biocompatibility and photostability and have been applied in biological imaging and sensor design for its great potential [14,15].

In our research, the redox and enzyme dual-stimuli responsive DDS (HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA) based on HMSN was developed as shown in Scheme 1 . In this system, the HMSN was synthesized by a selective etching method. The amino-abundant CDPEI were prepared using polyethylenimine (PEI) as the carbon source via hydrothermal method. The CDPEI with diameter being around ca. 5nm could block the pores of HMSN and endow the DDS with fluorescence property. The prepared CDPEI were connected to the surface of HMSN by disulfide bonds and served as gatekeepers to block the drug within the hollow cavity. Disulfide bonds between CDPEI and HMSN could be cleaved by GSH at high concentration in the release medium, resulting in the detachment of the gatekeepers from HMSN. The negative charged HA was coated on the surface of the carriers via the electrostatic interaction with the positive charged CDPEI to further control drug release. The HA not only acted as a gatekeeper but also a targeted ligand to enhance the cellular uptake by tumor cells. DOX was chosen as a model drug to be loaded into this system and the drug release experiment was conducted to confirm the dual-stimuli responsive release in the presence of both GSH and HAase. The hemolysis assay and stability test were performed to elevate the biocompatibility and safety of the system we constructed. The confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) and flow cytometry (FCM) were performed to investigate the cellular uptake and targeting ability by using A549 cells and NIH-3T3 cells. And the ability CDPEI serving as the real-imaging agent to track the nanoparticles in tumor cells was also investigated. Finally, the cytotoxicity of DOX-loaded HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA was conducted towards A549 cells and NIH-3T3 cells.

3-mercaptopropyltrimethoxy silane (MPTMS, 95%), 2,2′-Dipyridyl disulfide, glutathione (GSH), Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), 3-mercaptopropionic acid, sodium hyaluronic acid (molecular weight ca. 200kDa), hyaluronidase (HAase, ≥300U/mg), polyethylenimine (PEI, Mw=25,000), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and DOX were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Inc. (Shanghai, China). 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Amresco (USA). Cell culture media DMEM, penicillin-streptomycin, fetal bovine serum (FBS) were supplied by GIBCO, Invitrogen Co. (Carlsbad, USA). All other chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification.

100mg PEI was firstly dissolved in 20mL deionized water with the concentration of 5mg/mL. Then 400mg citric acid was added to the above solution and stirred for 15min. Then the mixed solution was stored in an autoclave and heated for 10h at 180°C with the heating rate of 3°C/min. The resulted solution was centrifuged at 15000rpm for the removal of the large particles and the supernatant was purified by dialysis (Mw=3500) for 6h.

The HMSN nanoparticles were prepared by the selective etching process according to the published work [16]. First, solid SiO2 nanospheres were made by a modified Stober method. In brief, 6mL of TEOS were added into a mixed solution of 74mL of ethanol, 10mL of deionized water, and 25–28% NH3·H2O (3.15mL). Then the mixture was stirred at 30°C for 1h to obtain colloidal silica suspension. The solid SiO2 nanospheres were centrifuged and dried under a vacuum. Then, the mesoporous silica shell was coated on the surface of solid SiO2 spheres. 300mg solid SiO2 were dispersed in 60mL water by ultra-sonication for 0.5h. Then the colloidal suspension was mixed with a mixture containing 450mg CTAB, 90mL water, 90mL ethanol, and 1.65mL NH3·H2O (25–28%). After stirring for 30min, 0.75mL TEOS was added rapidly. After stirring for 6h, the nanoparticles were gathered by centrifugation, and then redispersed in 60mL deionized water. 1.272g anhydrous Na2CO3 were added to the suspension with violent stirring. After the reaction maintaining at 50°C for 10h, the products were isolated by centrifugation and rinsed with water and ethanol. The thiol-functionalized HMSN (HMSN-SH) was prepared via post-grafting method with the ratio of HMSN and MPTMS being at 4:1(m/v) according to the reported literature [16].

The 2-carboxyethyl 2-pyridyl disulfide (Py-SS-COOH) was synthesized according to the reported research [17]. 500mg HMSN-SH was first dispersed in 60mL volumes of ethanol, followed by the addition of 100mg Py-SS-COOH. The solution was stirred at room temperature for >36h. And the as-prepared HMSN-SS-COOH nanoparticles were centrifuged to collect, then washed with ethanol and dried under vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading process and the dual-stimuli responsive drug release
ce:section.ce:para: 20mg DOX was first dissolved in distilled water at the concentration of 5mg/mL. 20mg HMSN-SS-COOH was added to the DOX solution, and sonicated for 30min. Then, 4mL PBS7.4 was added dropwise to the mixture and stirred for 12h at room temperature. The DOX-loaded HMSN-SS-COOH was denoted as HMSN-SS-COOH/DOX. The HMSN-SS-COOH/DOX was centrifuged and washed with pH7.4 PBS to remove the DOX adsorbed on the surface of HMSN-SS-COOH. Subsequently, HMSN-SS-COOH/DOX was dispersed in 30mL pH7.4 PBS and then activated by 100mg EDC and 80mg NHS for 1h. And 20mg CDPEI was added to the solution. The mixed solution was further stirred for 12h. At last, 15mg HA was dissolved in pH7.4 PBS. And then, DOX-loaded HMSN-SS-CDPEI was added to the HA solution. The resultant solution was stirred for 24h. The DOX-loaded HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA nanoparticles are denoted as DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA. The HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA nanoparticles without loaded DOX were obtained as the described above except the addition of DOX and removal of the drug adsorbed.

To evaluate the redox and enzyme dual-stimuli responsive release, ca. 2mg DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA sample was dispersed in 9mL different release media: pH7.4 PBS, pH7.4 PBS with 150UmL−1 HAase, pH7.4 PBS with 10mM GSH, pH7.4 PBS with both 10mM GSH and 150UmL−1 HAase, respectively, shaking at 130rpm (37°C). Subsequently, after the predetermined time, 2mL release solution was get out and replaced with the same volumes of fresh release medium. The amount of DOX released from the DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA was determined at the wavelength of 480nm. Meanwhile, the drug release experiment in pH5.0 PBS was conducted with the same as in pH7.4 PBS.

20mg HMSN and HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA were dispersed in 5mL deionized water and pH7.4 PBS, respectively. And then the nanoparticles were sonicated for 5min to obtain the uniform dispersed solutions. The photographs were taken to investigate the dispersibility and stability of HMSN and HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA.

The A549 and NIH-3T3 cells were cultured in DMEM cell culture medium supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin/streptomycin at 37°C in 5% CO2. Cellular uptakes of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA samples by NIH-3T3 and A549 cells were evaluated using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). The cells were seeded in the sterile round glass bottom dish at a density of 5×104 cells/well and incubated for 24h. Then HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA nanoparticles were added to the corresponding cell wells at a final concentration of 25μg/mL. After incubation a period of 2h at 37°C, the supernatant was removed and cells were washed three times with cold PBS and fixed with 4% formaldehyde. Subsequently, the DAPI was used to stain the nuclei. And the fixed cells were observed on a Leica DM-6000 CS microscope (Leica Instruments Inc., Wetzlar, Germany).

A quantitative evaluation of the cellular uptake was carried out on FCM. Each cell was seeded in 6-well plates at a density of 5×105 per well and incubated overnight. The cells were incubated with 100μg/mL HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA sample for 2h at 37°C or 4°C. Then, the supernatant was removed and cells were washed three times with cold PBS and treated with trypsin. Subsequently, the cells were centrifuged at 1500rpm for 5min and resuspended in 400μL of PBS. The mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) was measured by collecting the 10,000 gated cells by FCM. In the competition experiments, 1mg/mL HA was pre-incubated with the A549 cells for 1h before the 100μg/mL HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA was added.

The A549 cells and NIH-3T3 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 2×104 cells/well and incubated for 24h, respectively. The culture medium was then sucked out and replaced by blank carriers and DOX-loaded samples containing different concentrations of DOX (0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0μg/mL). After an incubation of 24h, 50μL of MTT solution (2mg/mL) was added to the cell culture medium and incubated for 4h at 37°C to quantify cell viability. Then, the culture medium containing MTT was removed and 150μL DMSO was added into each well to dissolve the produced formazan. Finally, the absorbance of 96-well plates was measured at 570nm by a microplate reader.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F30, FEI, Netherlands) was used to observe the mesoporous structure and morphology of nanoparticles. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area (SBET) and Barrett–Joyner–Halenda pore distribution were measured by using a Nitrogen adsorption–desorption Surface area analyzer at −196°C (V-Sorb 2800P, Gold APP Instrument Corporation, China). The thermogravimetric analysis was used to carried out on TGA-50 (Shimadzu, Japan) under the nitrogen flow of 40mL/min at the heating rate of 5°C/min. Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometric (FT-IR) spectra was measured on a Bruker IFS (Switzerland) by using the KBr pellet technique. The Zeta potential was characterized on a Nano-ZS90 Nanosizer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using the TGA-50 instrument (Shimadzu, Japan) to study the weight loss after each step of modification with a heating rate of 5°C/min under nitrogen flow. The fluorescence spectrum was measured on a Hitachi F-4500 (Tokyo, Japan) fluorescence spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by using an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with an Al-Kα X-ray source.

The CDPEI was prepared by the hydrothermal reaction, in which the PEI was applied as the carbon source. As shown in Fig. 1A and B, the obtained CDPEI nanoparticles had excellent dispersibility and narrow size distribution with diameter being at 4–5nm. Considering their appropriate diameter, they could be served as gatekeepers to block the drug release from the nanocarriers. The high resolution TEM images (Fig. 1B) exhibited a crystalline structure of CDPEI. In addition, the XPS spectrum indicated that the percentages of C, O and N elements of CDPEI were 76.1%, 16.6% and 7.3%, respectively. The XRD pattern of CDPEI presented a wide diffraction peak at 2θ=21.4°, demonstrating that the physical state of CDPEI was amorphous carbon phase [18].

In Fig. 2A, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDPEI showed an absorption peak at 340nm. And the fluorescence spectrum of CDPEI showed a maximum excitation at 360nm and a maximum emission at 450nm. As can be seen in the inserted image of Fig. 2A, the prepared CDPEI presented the bright blue light under UV light irradiation with excitation wavelength of 365nm, which was corresponding to its emission spectrum. In addition, as shown in Fig. 2B, the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of CDPEI were measured to evaluate the fluorescence property of CDPEI under different excitation wavelengths. The CDPEI did not possess the excitation-dependent emission property as previously reported CDs [19] did. When the solution of CDPEI was excited at wavelength ranging from 300 to 420nm, the maximum emission wavelength was still at 460nm, which indicated that there was no multi-fluorescence center [20].

The preparation process of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA with CDPEI as a “gatekeeper” connected by disulfide bonds is illustrated in Scheme 1. Firstly, the HMSN-SH carriers were prepared by a post grafting process by using MPTMS as the coupling reagent, and then the HMSN-SH was allowed to react with Py-SS-COOH to obtain HMSN-SS-COOH by the disulfide bond exchange reaction. Subsequently, the positive charge CDPEI was covalently grafted onto the surfaces of HMSN by the amidation reaction. Finally, the polymer HA was coated on the surface of HMSN by the electrostatic attraction. As we can see from the TEM image (Fig. 3A), the average size of HMSN nanoparticles was around 230nm and the average thickness of the shell was about 80nm. And a uniform hollow structure and well-defined mesoporous shell could also be observed in the TEM image of HMSN. After grafting of CDPEI and coating of HA, the surface of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA (Fig. 3B) became rough compared with the smooth surface of HMSN, which suggested that the CDPEI and HA were successfully modified on the surface of HMSN.

The isotherms and pore size distributions of HMSN and HMSN-SH were characterized by N2 adsorption/desorption analysis as shown in Fig. 4 . The SBET, pore size distribution (DP) and total pore volume (Vt) are displayed in Table 1 . The SBET and Vt of bare HMSN were calculated to be 1034m2·g and 1.49cm3·g, respectively, and the DP was about 3.6nm. After modification of mercapto groups, the Dp of HMSN-SH was still 3.6nm, indicating that the internal mesoporous structure was not blocked after the grafting of mercapto groups on the surface of HMSN. The SBET and Vt were gradually decreased after the thiol modification.

The successful preparation of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA was testified by different methods. The zeta potentials of HMSN-SH, HMSN-SS-COOH, HMSN-SS-CDPEI and HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA is presented in Fig. 5A. The zeta potential of HMSN-SH was −21.4mV. After HMSN-SH being reacted with Py-SS-COOH, the zeta potential of HMSN-SS-COOH further decreased to −37.7mV. After grafting of CDPEI, the zeta potential of HMSN-SS-CDPEI reversed to +38.6mV. Moreover, the zeta potential of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA reversed back to −29.7mV after the grafting of HA on the surface of HMSN, which indicated that HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA nanoparticles were successfully prepared. The FT-IR spectra of HMSN-SH, HMSN-SS-COOH, HMSN-SS-CDPEI and HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA are shown in Fig. 5B. Compared with HMSN-SH, for HMSN-SS-COOH, a peak at 1717cm−1 emerged due to the carboxyl group on the surface of HMSN-SS-COOH. After grafting of CDPEI, the peak at 1717cm−1 decreased owing to the reaction between the carboxyl group of HMSN-SS-COOH and the amino of CDPEI. In addition, after the coating of HA which has abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of HMSN-SS-CDPEI, the characteristic CO absorption peak belongs to carboxyl groups appeared at 1717cm−1 indicating the successful preparation of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA. Moreover, the thermogravimetric analysis was carried out to further verify the grafting amount of each step. The TGA curves of different samples were displayed in Fig. 5C. With the temperature ranging from 50 to 600°C, about 14% weight loss for HMSN-SH was observed, which ascribed to the mercapto groups on the surface of the HMSN. After terminal carboxyl group was grafted on HMSN-SH, an additional weight loss of about 9% was observed for HMSN-SS-COOH, which should be attributed to the carboxyl group. As for HMSN-SS-CDPEI, an additional weight loss of ca. 5% was observed due to the CDPEI grafted on the surface of HMSN-SS-COOH. The weight loss of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA achieved up to 35%, the additional weight loss ca. 9% compared with HMSN-SS-CDPEI should be attributed to the HA coated on the surface of the HMSN. All the results confirmed the successful grafting of HMSN after each step.

Despite HMSNs were potential carriers for drug delivery, their safe applications in many fields had been limited due to the severe aggregation and hemolysis phenomena [21]. The amino-abundant CDPEI was employed to control the drug release and track the nanoparticles in vivo. However, the hemolysis would happen when the cells exposed to the positive charged nanoparticles. Therefore, HA with negative charged was coated on the surface of the HMSN-SS-CDPEI to increase the biocompatibility and stability of the nanoparticles. The biocompatibility of the nanoparticles was investigated by the hemolysis assay. As shown in Fig. S1, the relatively high hemolysis percentages were observed for the HMSN and HMSN-SS-CDPEI. However, for HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA, negligible hemolysis can be seen at low concentrations and the hemolysis percentage remained <2% when the concentration of nanoparticles reached up to 1000μg/mL. In order to investigate the stability of nanoparticles, The HMSN and HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA were dispersed in distilled water and pH7.4 PBS at a concentration of 3mg/mL. As exhibited in Fig. 5D, the HMSN were stable in distilled water but flocculated in pH7.4 PBS. On the contrary, the dispersibility of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA in PBS was significantly improved after coating HA on the surface of HMSN. And the HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA nanoparticles were stable in distilled water and pH7.4 PBS for >12h. The excellent dispersibility of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA in distilled water and pH7.4 PBS should be ascribed to the steric hindrance and electrostatic repulsions among the stretched HA chains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The loading efficiency and redox and enzyme dual-triggered drug release
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the loading efficiency and release properties of DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA, the anticancer drug DOX was used as a model drug. The drug loading efficiency of DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA was 30.5%, which was larger than that of DOX loaded in the mesoporous silica carrier [11,22]. The relatively high loading capacity of HMSN was due to the high specific surface area, mesoporous structure, and especially the hollow cavity.

Stimuli-responsive drug release plays a significant role in drug delivery, thus the drug release from DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA was investigated in response to reductive and enzyme stimuli. As shown in Fig. 6 , DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA exhibited slower release of DOX in pH7.4 PBS compared with pH5.0 PBS, demonstrating its potential to minimize the undesirable release in normal physiological conditions. The release rate was relatively low in pH7.4 PBS since the electrostatic interaction between DOX and negative charged silica was much stronger compared with that in pH5.0 PBS [23]. Moreover, the cumulative release percentage of DOX from DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA in both pH7.4 PBS and pH5.0 PBS were increased with the addition of 10mM GSH, confirmed the redox-responsive release property of DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA. And the cumulative release of the DOX in the presence of GSH was twice as high as that in the absence of GSH in pH7.4 PBS and pH5.0 PBS. As shown in Scheme 2 , this redox-responsive drug release was due to the cleavage of the disulfide bonds under the high concentration of GSH, leading to the detachment of the CDPEI and HA from HMSN and accelerated release rate of DOX from DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA. Meanwhile, the release of DOX from DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA was further enhanced in the presence of HAase in both pH7.4 PBS and pH5.0 PBS, but the release rate enhancement induced by HAase was not as significant as that by GSH which could be explained as that even though the macromolecular HA on the surface was degraded into small fragments by HAase, the residual fragments of HA linked by disulfide bonds still existed on the surface of HMSN, which could slightly hinder the drug release from the mesopores of the carriers. Moreover, with the presence of dual stimuli (10mM GSH and HAase), DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA exhibited a faster release of DOX in both pH5.0 and pH7.4 PBS compared to that triggered by single stimuli probably due to the synergetic effect of GSH and HAase [11]. And the cumulative release of DOX under dual stimuli was about 2.5-fold higher than that in pH7.4 PBS and pH5.0 PBS alone. Clearly, DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA had the ability to achieve redox and enzyme dual-responsive drug release (Scheme 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro targeting analysis of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: CD44 receptors were over-expressed on the membrane of A549 cells but had a low expression on NIH-3T3 cells, so the A549 cells and NIH 3T3 cells were used as the receptor positive and negative control to investigate the targeting ability of nanoparticles. The flow cytometry (FCM) was used to quantitatively evaluate the cellular uptake of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA nanoparticles versus HMSN-SS-CDPEI nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 7A, for the HMSN-SS-CDPEI group, no significant difference in mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) was observed between A549 and NIH-3T3 cells, indicating that HMSN-SS-CDPEI do not possess targeting property towards A549 cells. In contrast, after treatment with HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA, the MFI of A549 cells was 2.87-fold higher in comparison to that of NIH-3T3 cells. Additionally, the MFI of HMSN-SS-CDPEI treated A549 cells was slightly lower than that of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA treated A549 cells, which might be due to the uptake promotion of HA grafted on the surface of the HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA nanoparticles. And the MFI of NIH-3T3 cells incubated with HMSN-SS-CDPEI was apparently stronger compared with that incubated with HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA, which was because that the fluorescence intensity of nanoparticles decreased after the grafting of HA. These results demonstrated that the cellular uptake of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA nanoparticles was mediated by CD44 receptors on A549 cells. In addition, the free HA and low temperature (4°C) were chosen to further testify the selective uptake of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA mediated by CD44 receptors on A549 cells. As displayed in Fig. 7B, the uptake of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA by A549 cells reduced sharply to about half of that after HA pretreatment, suggesting that the cellular uptake of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA was mainly mediated by CD44 receptors. Moreover, the cellular uptake of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA was markedly inhibited when the A549 cells were incubated with HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA at 4°C, since the receptor-mediated cellular uptake of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA by receptors over-expressed cells involves ATP-dependent endocytosis. These results indicated that the internalization of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA was mainly mediated by CD44 receptors.

In order to visualize the cellular uptake of the nanoparticles, confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was employed to observe the cellular uptake of particles by these two cell lines. As shown in Fig. 8 , the cells incubated with HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA nanoparticles could emit the blue, green and red fluorescence under excitation wavelengths of 408, 488 and 561nm, respectively. The amount of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA nanoparticles taken up by A549 cells was much more than that by NIH-3T3 cells, demonstrating that the HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA was preferentially taken up by A549 cells. The results confirmed the endocytosis of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA mediated by CD44 receptors.

The cell viability of different formulations at a series of DOX concentrations and the corresponding amount of blank nanocarriers towards cell lines of A549 and NIH-3T3 were evaluated by MTT assay. As shown in Fig. S2, the cell viability of A549 and NIH-3T3 cells incubated with blank HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA and HMSN-SH for 48h was above 80%, indicating that the nanocarriers had good compatibility. The results of the cytotoxic effect of the DOX-loaded nanocarriers were displayed in Fig. 9 . The cytotoxicity of different DOX preparations was dose-dependent for both A549 and NIH-3T3 cells. No significant difference of the cytotoxicity between A549 and NIH-3T3 cells was observed when they were incubated with free DOX for 48h, demonstrating that the free DOX do not have selectivity towards the two cells. On the contrary, after the treatment of DOX-loaded HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA, the cell viability of the CD44 receptors over-expressed A549 cells was lower than that of the receptors negative NIH-3T3 cells under the same DOX concentration. Besides, the cytotoxicity of DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA was higher than DOX/HMSN-SH for A549 cells, which could be ascribed to the fact that the promoted uptake of DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA mediated by the CD44 receptors could increase the antitumor activity of DOX. Meanwhile, when the receptors-negative NIH-3T3 cells were incubated with DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA, a much lower cytotoxicity was observed compared with free DOX and DOX/HMSN-SH, demonstrating low uptake of DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA by NIH-3T3 cells. Moreover, the IC50 values (the concentration at which 50% of cells were killed) were calculated by SPSS Statistics software and the results were given in Table 2 . The IC50 value of the DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA was much lower than that of the free DOX and DOX/HMSN-SH for A549 cells, demonstrating the excellent anticancer activity of the DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA against A549 cells. On the contrary, the IC50 value of the DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA was the highest in all formulations in the case of NIH-3T3 cells, demonstrating that the DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA nanocarriers could reduce the side-effects to normal cells. In addition, the IC50 value of DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA reduced from 2.829μg/mL for NIH-3T3 cells to 0.447μg/mL for A549 cells, which was in accordance with the results of the FCM, further confirming that the endocytosis of the DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA was mediated by the CD44 receptors. As a result, DOX/HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA nanocarriers could improve the anticancer activity of the released DOX in CD44 receptors over-expressed cancer cells and reduce the side-effects in normal cells.

In summary, a redox and enzyme dual-stimuli responsive targeted drug delivery system (HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA) based on HMSN was developed, in which the CDPEI served as both the imaging agents and gatekeepers. The positive charged CDPEI were grafted on the pore openings of HMSN through disulfide bonds. And the hyaluronic acid (HA) was further grafted on the surface of HMSN through electrostatic interaction with the positive charged CDPEI, to realize targeted drug delivery, controlled drug release and to improve biocompatibility of the nanoparticles. Anticancer drug DOX was chosen as a model drug to evaluate the dual-stimuli responsive drug release in the presence of the GSH and HAase. The drug release results manifested that both the GSH and HAase could accelerate the release rate of DOX from the nanocarriers, indicating that the DOX-loaded HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA possessed obvious dual-stimuli responsive property. In addition, the HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA exhibited excellent fluorescent property in vitro. CLSM and FCM experiment illustrated that HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA exhibited a higher cellular uptake by A549 cells than NIH-3T3 cells via the CD44 receptor-mediated endocytosis. The DOX-loaded HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA exhibited higher anticancer activity against A549 cells than NIH-3T3 cells. Our work suggested an exploration of dual-stimuli responsive targeted drug delivery system of HMSN-SS-CDPEI@HA as a promising platform for cancer treatment and real-time imaging.
We have no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81473165 and No. 81603058), the Doctoral Research Funding of Liaoning Province (No. 201601146) and the Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory of Drug Preparation Design & Evaluation of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (No. LZ2015068).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2017.04.059.
